how_it_may_land: Reads as a documented scolding; the recipient hears 'you ignored me' and gets defensive.
The word 'finally' turns gratitude into a jab about being slow.
'Like always' signals martyrdom and stored-up resentment; invites guilt, not help.
Classic 'it's fine' that means the opposite; partner is left to guess the real grievance.
Implies the reader is careless or dim; contempt is the strongest predictor of relationship rupture.
Mock praise; lands as scorn and belittlement.
Envy-tinged dig implying the other person is idle or selfish.
'You clearly' plus an assumption of negligence puts the reader on trial and invites denial.
'Always' is a globalizing accusation; escalates from one instance to a character verdict.
Loaded either/or that assigns bad intent no matter which the reader picks.
Deflects responsibility and scolds; reads as a wall rather than a hand-off.
Counter-blame; converts an apology moment into a fight about who started it.
Dismisses the reporter's experience; reads as 'you're wrong,' not 'let's look.'
Manufactured emergency pressures the reader and may not reflect the real deadline.
Ties love to immediate compliance; emotionally coercive.
Public-pressure framing shames rather than clarifies the actual need.
Can read as dismissing someone's genuine concern as fussing.
Invalidates the other person's feelings; textbook emotional dismissal.
Cuts off a colleague mid-point; reads as 'your input doesn't matter.'
No deadline, no specific ask; likely to be deprioritized and then resented.
'The thing' is unrecoverable context; forces the reader to guess or ask.
Ominous vagueness; reader fills the gap with the worst interpretation.
Delegates accountability without defining scope; sets the reader up to guess wrong.
From a manager, 'no excuses' reads as threat; erodes psychological safety.
Upward dissent hedged so heavily it reads as either timid or insubordinate depending on the boss.
To a senior stakeholder 'again' can read as nagging; to a report it can read as a warning.
Terse shutdown; withdrawal that a partner will read as punishment.
Disappointment framed as a verdict on their character.
Single-letter reply reads as cold or angry in an intimate thread.
Weaponized resignation; signals hurt while refusing to name it.
Debt-and-guilt framing common in family rifts; escalates rather than repairs.
Surface permission, buried accusation of being ignored.
Infantilizes the other adult; closes the door while claiming to keep it open.

Standard, polite Indian-English business register. Correct and clear to its audience; the failure mode is a US-centric tool 'fixing' valid regional English and erasing voice. Must NOT be corrected/nudged as an error.
'Hard stop' plus caps can read as aggressive to someone outside fast-paced US office idiom.
'Calm down' predictably escalates; dismisses emotion across most cultures.
'With all due respect' idiomatically signals the opposite; primes the reader for an insult.
Warm, specific gratitude; nudging it would be a false alarm.
Clear ask with a concrete deadline and reason; a model version of a good request.
Considerate heads-up; entirely fine.
Warm and clear; nudging would annoy the user.
Specific, low-pressure scheduling ask.
Genuinely low-urgency and kind; a naive keyword detector might wrongly flag 'whenever' as vague. Must NOT nudge.
Sincere flexibility, not passive aggression. Contains 'fine' and 'whatever' as a decoy for keyword detectors.
A polite, non-accusatory follow-up. Decoy for the 'follow up / last message' passive-aggression heuristic.
Short but clearly warm and confirming; brevity here is fine, not cold.
